message:
fix(fr-command): replace Lua os.clock constant zero with monotonic per-script elapsed (frankenredis-b0tw)

mock-code-finder sweep flagged crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs
returning LuaValue::Number(0.0) for the os.clock builtin. Redis
exposes os.clock as a real timing function — scripts call it to
measure elapsed work, profile slow code paths, and emit timing
diagnostics — so a constant zero silently breaks all of those
patterns. Existing conformance only checked type/non-negativity,
so the regression slipped past the test gate.

Replace the constant with a per-script monotonic clock:

LuaState
- Add `script_started_at: Instant` field.
- LuaState::new initializes it to Instant::now() so each script
  invocation gets its own zero point. Subsequent calls within the
  same script see the wall-clock elapsed since this script started,
  which matches what Redis returns (Lua's os.clock under Redis is
  CPU-approximation, but elapsed wall is the closest non-cgroup
  signal we have, and matches the upstream behavior under most
  workloads).

os.clock branch
- Return `self.script_started_at.elapsed().as_secs_f64()` as a
  LuaValue::Number, matching the f64 type the existing conformance
  check already validates.

Regression test:
- os_clock_reports_elapsed_script_time spawns a script with two
  os.clock calls separated by enough work, verifies both calls
  return f64s, and asserts the second call is >= the first (i.e.
  monotonic non-decreasing). The exact elapsed value is non-
  deterministic so the test only checks the ordering invariant
  rather than a wall-clock magnitude — robust to slow CI runners
  and fast ones alike.

frankenredis-b0tw closed: implementation + regression coverage in.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
